title: Sequential Circuits
summary: Flip-flops, latches, and registers — circuits that remember state across clock cycles.

sections.heading: Why Sequential Logic?
sections.body: Combinational circuits cannot store history. Sequential circuits add memory elements so outputs depend on present inputs AND past state. This enables counters, state machines, pipelines, and any design that operates over time.
bullets: Present state + inputs → next state + outputs

sections.heading: Latches vs Flip-Flops
sections.body: A latch is level-sensitive: transparent while enable is active. A flip-flop is edge-triggered: samples inputs only on clock edge (rising or falling). Synthesis for FPGAs/ASICs strongly prefers edge-triggered D flip-flops.
bullets: SR latch: set-reset, can hold state
D latch: Q follows D when EN=1
D flip-flop: Q ← D on active clock edge
JK and T flip-flops: specialized toggle/count behavior

callout.text: Avoid latches in RTL unless intentional. Incomplete if/case statements infer latches in synthesis.

sections.heading: Flip-Flops in Verilog
sections.body: Use always_ff (SystemVerilog) or always @(posedge clk) for sequential logic. Non-blocking assignment (<=) schedules updates at end of time step — critical for correct simulation of registers.
bullets: Synchronous reset: if (rst) q <= 0; inside clocked block

sections.heading: Registers and Shift Registers
sections.body: A register is a bank of flip-flops storing a multi-bit value. Shift registers move data serially — used in UART, CRC, and scan chains. Parallel-load shift registers combine both modes.
bullets: n-bit register: n D flip-flops sharing clock
SIPO/PISO: serial-in parallel-out and vice versa
Universal shift register: configurable direction and load

sections.heading: Counters
sections.body: Binary counters increment (or decrement) on each clock edge. Synchronous counters share one clock; asynchronous (ripple) counters chain flip-flop outputs — simpler but slower carry propagation.

quiz.question: A D flip-flop updates its output on:
quiz.explanation: Edge-triggered flip-flops sample D on rising or falling clock edge.

quiz.question: Latches are ___ sensitive; flip-flops are ___ triggered.
quiz.explanation: Latches respond to enable level; flip-flops to clock edges.

quiz.question: In Verilog sequential blocks, use:
quiz.explanation: Non-blocking <= models end-of-time-step updates like real flip-flops.

quiz.question: An 8-bit register contains:
quiz.explanation: One flip-flop per bit, sharing clock (and typically reset).

quiz.question: T flip-flop toggles when:
quiz.explanation: T=1 causes Q to toggle; T=0 holds state.

quiz.question: Incomplete case in combinational always block may infer:
quiz.explanation: Synthesis infers latches when outputs must hold previous values — usually unintended.

flashcards.back: Q⁺ = D (next state equals D input at clock edge)
Async: immediate when reset asserted; Sync: only on clock edge
Minimum time D must be stable BEFORE clock edge
Serial In, Parallel Out shift register
